title: Measuring economic activity (GDP)
summary:
## The big idea Before you can grow, stabilise or compare economies, you have to *measure* them. The headline measure is **GDP** — and the single most useful insight in this chapter is that an economy's **output, income and expenditure are the same number** seen from three angles. Everything you spend is someone's income; everything earned was paid for producing something: > **National output = National income = National expenditure** ## The circular flow of income That three-way identity comes from the **circular flow**: households supply factors of production to firms and receive incomes; they spend those incomes on the firms' output, and round it goes.
**What each part means:** the two orange arrows are the core loop — households' **spending** flows to firms (top), and firms pay it back out as **incomes** — wages, rent, interest and profit (bottom). The yellow arrow shows **injections** entering the loop: investment (I), government spending (G) and exports (X) add spending that didn't come from current household income. The red arrow shows **withdrawals (leakages)**: saving (S), taxes (T) and imports (M) take spending out. When **injections > withdrawals** the flow — national income — grows; when they are equal, the economy is in equilibrium.
## GDP and its relatives - **GDP (Gross Domestic Product):** the total value of goods and services produced **within a country's borders** in a period, whoever owns the factories. - **GNI (Gross National Income):** GDP **plus net income from abroad** (profits, interest and wages flowing in, minus those flowing out). The gap matters: the Philippines and India receive huge worker **remittances** (GNI > GDP), while Ireland hosts multinationals whose profits leave (GDP ≫ GNI). ## Real vs nominal — strip out inflation **Nominal GDP** is measured at today's prices, so it rises when *either* output *or* prices rise. **Real GDP** is measured at constant (base-year) prices, isolating genuine output change. The rough rule: > **real growth ≈ nominal growth − inflation** If nominal GDP grows 12% while inflation is 9%, the economy really grew about 3%. An economy can have booming nominal GDP and *shrinking* real output — always check which one a data table shows. And divide by population: **GDP per capita** is what tracks the average person's slice. ## Comparing GDP across countries and time — the caveat list This list is a ready-made evaluation paragraph; learn it: - **Population** — use per-capita figures, not totals. - **Inflation** — use real, not nominal. - **Price levels differ across countries** — convert at **Purchasing Power Parity (PPP)**, not market exchange rates: \$1,000 buys far more in Cairo than in Zurich. - **Distribution** — an average says nothing about who gets what; a rising mean can hide stagnant medians. - **The informal/hidden economy** — street vendors, subsistence farming, cash-in-hand and black-market work go unrecorded, badly understating output in many developing economies. - **Non-marketed output** — childcare at home, DIY and volunteering are real production but never counted. - **What GDP ignores** — leisure, working hours and conditions, environmental damage, health. A country can raise GDP by cutting down its forests and lengthening the working week. ## GDP and living standards Higher real GDP per head *usually* raises **material living standards** — more goods, better services. But it is a means, not the whole of **welfare**: it says nothing about how income is shared, how long and hard people work, or the state of the environment. That is why broader indicators (like the **HDI**, developed at A2) sit alongside it. The safe exam formulation: *rising real GDP per capita is associated with higher living standards, but the link is loose and the caveats matter*. ## Why it matters Every macro objective — growth targets, the trade cycle, output gaps — is defined in terms of real GDP, and every data-response paper throws GDP tables at you. The marks go to students who reflexively ask: *real or nominal? total or per head? PPP-adjusted? and how big is the informal economy here?*
## ⚡ Quick revision - **Output = income = expenditure** — three views of one flow (circular flow). - Injections **I + G + X** vs withdrawals **S + T + M**; flow grows when injections exceed withdrawals. - **GDP** = produced *within borders*; **GNI** = GDP + net income from abroad (remittances → GNI > GDP). - **Real = nominal − inflation** (approx.); compare over time with **real GDP per capita**. - Across countries: adjust for **PPP**; beware the **informal economy**. - GDP misses: distribution, non-marketed work, leisure, environment → living standards ≠ welfare. - **HDI** and friends fill some gaps (A2 development).

keyTerms.definition: The total value of output produced within a country's borders in a year.
GDP plus net income earned from abroad.
GDP measured at current prices, not adjusted for inflation.
GDP measured at constant prices, adjusted for inflation.
GDP divided by population; a guide to average living standards.
An adjustment that compares incomes using the local cost of living.
Unrecorded economic activity that causes GDP to understate true output.
The flow of income between households and firms, with injections and withdrawals.

diagrams: **Circular flow:** households and firms with factor payments and spending flowing between them; show injections (I, G, X) entering and withdrawals (S, T, M) leaving.
Optional: a bar/line comparison of **real vs nominal GDP** to illustrate the effect of inflation.
tips: Always compare **real GDP per capita** over time — mention both the inflation and population adjustments.
For international comparisons, bring in **PPP** and the **informal economy** as key caveats.
Separate **living standards (material)** from **welfare/quality of life** — GDP measures the former imperfectly and ignores much of the latter.
Use **GNI/remittances** as an application point for economies like the **Philippines or India** (large overseas worker incomes), and **Ireland** (GDP > GNI due to multinationals).
List GDP's **limitations** as a ready-made evaluation paragraph (distribution, externalities, hidden economy, non-marketed output).

channels.why: Clear GDP, real vs nominal and circular-flow explanations.

channels.name: EconplusDal
channels.why: Good on GDP limitations and living standards.

revisionNotes:
# Measuring economic activity — quick revision

**The identity**
- National **output = income = expenditure** — the circular flow guarantees it.
- Injections: **I + G + X**. Withdrawals: **S + T + M**. Injections > withdrawals → national income grows.

**The measures**
- **GDP**: value of output produced *within the country's borders*.
- **GNI**: GDP + **net income from abroad** — remittance economies (Philippines, India): GNI > GDP; multinational hubs (Ireland): GDP ≫ GNI.
- **GDP per capita** = GDP ÷ population.

**Real vs nominal**
- Nominal = current prices (output *and* inflation). Real = constant prices.
- **Real growth ≈ nominal growth − inflation.** Always compare in real per-capita terms.

**Comparison caveats (evaluation paragraph)**
1. Population → per capita. 2. Inflation → real. 3. **PPP** for cross-country price differences. 4. Distribution hidden by averages. 5. **Informal economy** unrecorded. 6. Non-marketed output (home care, DIY). 7. Ignores leisure, environment, working conditions.

**Living standards**
- Real GDP/head tracks *material* living standards loosely; welfare needs broader measures (HDI at A2).
questions:
# Practice questions — Measuring economic activity **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 1 style — multiple choice **1.** A country's nominal GDP rises by 10% in a year in which inflation is 4%. Real GDP has risen by approximately: - A — 14% - B — 10% - C — 6% - D — 4%
▸ Show answer

C. Real growth ≈ nominal growth − inflation = 10% − 4% = 6%. The other options add, ignore or swap the adjustment.

**2.** A country whose citizens work abroad in large numbers and send money home is likely to have: - A — GDP greater than GNI - B — GNI greater than GDP - C — GDP equal to GNI - D — negative GDP
▸ Show answer

B. Remittances are income earned abroad flowing in: GNI = GDP + net income from abroad, so GNI > GDP (e.g. the Philippines).

**3.** In the circular flow of income, which of the following is an injection? - A — household saving - B — spending on imported cars - C — a firm's purchase of new machinery - D — income tax payments
▸ Show answer

C. Investment (with G and X) injects spending into the flow. Saving, imports and taxes are the three withdrawals (S, T, M).

**4.** A large informal economy means that official GDP statistics will: - A — overstate the country's true output - B — understate the country's true output - C — be unaffected - D — equal GNI
▸ Show answer

B. Unrecorded market activity — street trading, cash work, subsistence farming — is real production that never enters the accounts, so measured GDP understates true output.

## Paper 2 style — data response > Country A reports GDP per capita of \$14,000; Country B reports \$9,000. A development agency notes: B's informal sector is estimated at 35% of output against A's 10%; prices for everyday goods are about 40% lower in B; B's income distribution is markedly more equal; and B's figure is growing 6% a year in real terms against A's 1%. **(a)** Define 'GDP per capita'. **[2]** **(b)** Explain why economists prefer real GDP to nominal GDP when measuring growth. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse **two** reasons why the raw figures may overstate the gap in living standards between A and B. **[6]** **(d)** Assess whether the average resident of Country A is better off than the average resident of Country B. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) GDP divided by population — average output/income per person. [1+1]

(b) Nominal mixes output growth with inflation; real GDP values output at constant prices, isolating the true change in production. Example helps. [4]

(c) (i) B's larger informal economy (35% vs 10%) means far more of B's true output goes unmeasured; (ii) prices 40% lower in B → at PPP, B's \$9,000 buys much more than at market rates; also distribution — B's mean is closer to its median. Any two, developed. [3+3]

(d) For A: still likely higher measured material standard. For B: PPP + informal adjustments narrow or close the gap; more equal distribution means the typical person does better relative to the mean; faster growth means the ranking may soon flip. Judgement: 'better off' depends on adjustment and on trend vs level. [8]

## Paper 2 / 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain, using the circular flow of income, why national output, income and expenditure are equal, and how injections and withdrawals affect the size of the flow. **[8]** **(b)** Assess whether GDP is a good measure of a country's standard of living. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Households ↔ firms loop; every purchase is someone's income for producing output; injections I+G+X expand the flow, withdrawals S+T+M shrink it; equilibrium when equal.

(b) For: correlates with consumption, health, education; comparable and timely. Against: distribution, informal and non-marketed output, leisure/hours, environment, PPP issues. Judgement: good first approximation, must be supplemented (real, per capita, PPP, HDI).

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain the difference between GDP and GNI, and why the gap is large for some countries. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether comparisons of GDP per capita between developed and developing economies are meaningful. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Borders vs residents' income; net income from abroad; remittance economies (GNI > GDP) vs multinational hubs like Ireland (GDP > GNI).

(b) Problems: exchange rates vs PPP, informal sector size, data quality, basket differences, distribution. Defence: PPP-adjusted per-capita data still reveal huge, real differences; trends are informative. Judgement: meaningful if properly adjusted and treated as approximate.
